items.question: Who uses a confusion matrix in ML workflows?
items.answer: Data scientists, ML engineers, and product owners use it after choosing a classification threshold — to report concrete false alarms (FP) and misses (FN) on validation or test data before deployment.

items.question: What is a binary confusion matrix?
items.answer: A 2×2 count table with cells TP, FP, TN, and FN — tallying how many examples were true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives at one fixed cutoff comparing predictions to ground truth.

items.question: When should you publish a confusion matrix instead of only AUC?
items.answer: When a deployment threshold is chosen (or about to be chosen) and stakeholders need interpretable error counts — false alarms vs missed positives — not threshold-free ranking summaries.

items.question: Where do TP, FP, TN, and FN sit in the standard layout?
items.answer: With rows = actual and columns = predicted (sklearn): TN and FP are the actual-0 row; FN and TP are the actual-1 row. TP is bottom-right when positive class is 1.

items.question: Why report FP and FN separately rather than only accuracy?
items.answer: FP (false alarm) and FN (miss) have different business costs and map to different fixes — accuracy merges all four cells into one number and hides minority-class failure modes.

items.question: How do you compute TP, FP, TN, FN from arrays in Python?
items.answer: On held-out y_true and hard y_pred with positive=1: TP = sum((y_true==1)&(y_pred==1)), FP = sum((y_true==0)&(y_pred==1)), TN = sum((y_true==0)&(y_pred==0)), FN = sum((y_true==1)&(y_pred==0)); or use sklearn.metrics.confusion_matrix.

items.question: Confusion matrix vs accuracy — what does the matrix add?
items.answer: Accuracy is (TP+TN)/N — one scalar. The matrix exposes all four counts so you see whether errors are mostly FP (false alarms) or FN (misses), which accuracy alone cannot distinguish.

items.question: Confusion matrix at one threshold vs ROC-AUC — when prefer each?
items.answer: Use ROC-AUC to compare models before the cutoff is known (threshold-free ranking). Use the confusion matrix after you fix a threshold to report operational FP, FN, TPR, and FPR at that point.

items.question: False positive vs false negative — how do they differ in the matrix?
items.answer: FP: actual negative, predicted positive (false alarm) — top-right in sklearn layout. FN: actual positive, predicted negative (miss) — bottom-left. Swapping which class is 'positive' relabels which errors are FP vs FN.

items.question: If every example is predicted negative, what happens to TP and FP?
items.answer: TP = 0 and FP = 0 — no positive predictions. TN equals all actual negatives; FN equals all actual positives. Accuracy can still look high if negatives dominate.

items.question: If you lower the classification threshold, what typically happens to TP and FP?
items.answer: More examples are predicted positive — TP usually rises (catch more positives) and FP usually rises too (more false alarms). FN falls. The matrix shifts toward more mass in the predicted-1 column.

items.question: Why must TP + FP + TN + FN equal the dataset size N?
items.answer: Each labeled example has one true class and receives one hard prediction — it belongs to exactly one of the four mutually exclusive cells, so the counts partition the evaluation set.

items.question: What if you swap which class is labeled positive without changing predictions?
items.answer: The numeric cells relabel — what was TP may become TN, FP and FN swap roles. TPR, precision, and error interpretation change; always document positive=1 before reporting.

items.question: What if you build the matrix on training data instead of a holdout set?
items.answer: Counts are optimistically biased — FP and FN will likely increase on fresh data. Stakeholders will see more false alarms and misses in production than the training matrix suggested.

items.question: Cause: Severe class imbalance with a naive majority-class classifier. Effect on the confusion matrix?
items.answer: TN and accuracy grow large; TP may be small and FN may capture most positives — the matrix shows near-zero TPR even when accuracy exceeds 95%.

items.question: Cause: Stricter threshold (higher cutoff on P(positive)). Effect on FP and FN?
items.answer: Fewer positive predictions — FP usually drops (fewer false alarms) while FN usually rises (more missed positives). TP may fall if some true positives scored below the new cutoff.
